# YC W26 Application, Lexform

## Q: What is your company going to make? [max=500 chars]
Lexform is a CRM and intake platform for solo and 2-3 person law firms in the US, starting with personal injury. Most solo lawyers run intake on phone calls and email, lose 30 to 40% of leads to slow follow-up, and pay $300 to $900 per signed case to legal-vertical lead vendors. We replace the spreadsheet-and-Outlook stack with a workflow that auto-routes inbound leads, drafts the first three follow-up messages, and reports per-source signed-case yield.

## Q: How far along are you? [max=400 chars]
Live since November 2025. 14 paying firms at $199/mo on annual plans, $33,432 ARR as of last week. We onboarded 11 of those 14 in the last 60 days, mostly through a Texas Trial Lawyers Association referral. Two firms have churned (one closed; one switched to Clio). Net retention 100% on the 12 still on. Self-served deployment in 2 hours.

## Q: Why did you pick this idea to work on? [max=700 chars]
My co-founder Sam ran intake at his father's three-lawyer PI firm in Houston for four years before law school. He watched the firm pay $470 average per signed case to a major lead vendor, then lose roughly a third of those leads because nobody called back inside 60 minutes. Existing CRMs (Clio, MyCase) are case-management tools that retrofitted intake; none of them solve the speed-to-call problem on inbound. Sam already had the customer relationships. I had built two CRUD-with-workflow products before. The failure mode is a phone-call-timing problem, not an AI problem. The wedge is narrow and obvious.

## Q: Who are your competitors, and what do you understand that they don't? [max=700 chars]
Clio (case management; not intake-native), MyCase (same pattern), Lawmatics (closest competitor; sales-team CRM repositioned for law). Lawmatics is our real competition. They charge $349/mo on annual contracts, target firms of 5+ lawyers, and require a 2-week implementation. Solo PI lawyers will not sit through a 2-week implementation. What we understand: solos do not need a configurable workflow engine; they need a 2-hour setup that works for the next 18 months without changes. We compete on time-to-value, not feature surface area, and we will lose if we let feature requests pull us into Lawmatics' positioning.

## Q: What is the hard part of this business? [max=600 chars]
The hard part is not the product. The hard part is paid acquisition CAC for solo legal: legal-vertical Google ads run $40 to $90 per click and we have measured legal-CRM landing-page conversion at 1.2 to 2.1%. That gives us a paid CAC ceiling around $2K to $7K per signed customer, which against a $2,388 ACV means paid acquisition does not work standalone. We have to win on referrals and bar-association partnerships in years 1 and 2; if we cannot, we will not get to a paid-acquisition-defensible position before larger competitors notice the segment.
